label: premortem-position
system:
You are performing one specific cognitive operation as an isolated component of a legal-exploration pipeline. You never see the other components' outputs, and nobody sees yours until a separate neutral aggregation step. Ground rules for every component:
- The scenario is an invented hypothetical; there is no real client. Your output goes to a licensed California attorney who will do the actual legal reasoning. You generate angles to pursue and things to verify — not advice to be relied on.
- Cite specific authorities (statutes, cases, regulations) when they genuinely support a point. Every citation is automatically flagged UNVERIFIED for human checking, so cite what you believe is correct, but never fabricate quotations or pinpoint holdings you are unsure of. When unsure, write "verify whether X holds Y" instead of asserting it.
- Perform ONLY the operation described below. Do not produce a general-purpose legal memo.

Operation: state, in a few sentences, the position or course of action the
agency's counsel would MOST LIKELY adopt here. Not the best position — the
likely one. No analysis beyond what is needed to state it.
prompt:
## Facts (invented hypothetical — no real parties)

The City of Piedra Blanca (invented) is renegotiating its 15-year exclusive solid-waste franchise, worth roughly $180M over its term. A consultant for one bidder, GreenCycle Environmental, created a Signal group called "PB Zero Waste Future" and added three of the five councilmembers, several GreenCycle executives, and two community advocates. Disappearing messages were set to one week. Over three months, participants discussed franchise terms, diversion targets, "what council will realistically accept," and the timing of the RFP release. The members participated from personal phones; nothing was ever forwarded to city accounts. One councilmember, uneasy, screenshotted about 40 exchanges before they auto-deleted; the rest are gone.

After losing the eventual award to GreenCycle, competing bidder Pacific Refuse submitted a CPRA request for "all communications by any councilmember concerning the franchise, on any platform or account." The city clerk searched city systems and responded "no responsive records." Pacific Refuse has now sued for declaratory and injunctive relief under the CPRA, and separately demanded the city preserve and produce the surviving screenshots. The city's retention schedule, adopted under Government Code § 34090, requires retention of correspondence relating to franchises for four years.

## The question

Are the Signal messages "public records" under City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608 even though they sat on personal devices and most no longer exist? What search and inquiry did the CPRA obligate the city to conduct before answering "no responsive records," and did the clerk's answer violate the Act? Does the auto-deletion violate § 34090 or any retention duty as applied to records on personal accounts, and what remedies exist for records that are gone (the CPRA has no damages provision) — declaratory relief, fees, adverse findings in the franchise litigation, referral? Do the three members' communications in a shared group about franchise terms create Brown Act exposure that interacts with the CPRA claim? What should the city do now, and what policy should it adopt going forward?

What position would the agency's counsel most likely adopt?

likely_position.description: the position/course of action a cautious public-agency counsel would most likely recommend, in 2-4 sentences

text: {"likely_position":"Counsel would most likely avoid contesting the core San Jose point and instead assume, for risk-management purposes, that at least some of the Signal content (franchise terms, RFP timing) qualifies as a \"public record\" under the content/context test of City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608, since it plainly relates to \"the conduct of the public's business.\" Counsel would likely concede that the clerk's search was legally inadequate — San Jose requires an inquiry to individual officials about potentially responsive material on personal accounts/devices, not just a search of city systems — and would recommend promptly curing this by directing councilmembers to search personal devices, producing the 40 surviving screenshots (redacted as needed for privilege/deliberative process claims that counsel would likely assert are weak but worth raising), and issuing a supplemental CPRA response rather than defending the \"no responsive records\" answer through litigation. On the deleted messages, counsel would resist framing auto-deletion as a knowing/willful § 34090 violation (arguing the retention schedule's applicability to individual officials' personal-device ephemeral messaging is legally unsettled and the councilmembers' conduct was not city action), while still opening a litigation hold now, quietly documenting good faith, and treating any adverse-inference or spoliation argument in the franchise litigation as a settlement-pressure factor rather than something to fight to a published ruling. Counsel would likely recommend settling/mooting the CPRA suit (supplemental production, stipulated declaratory relief, negotiated fees under Gov. Code § 7923.115) rather than litigating San Jose's scope to judgment, refer the three-councilmember group chat for a quiet internal Brown Act compliance review (without volunteering a serial-meeting admission), and adopt going forward a personal-device/ephemeral-messaging and records-retention policy — all aimed at containing exposure and optics rather than vindicating the city's original \"no responsive records\" position."}

data.likely_position: Counsel would most likely avoid contesting the core San Jose point and instead assume, for risk-management purposes, that at least some of the Signal content (franchise terms, RFP timing) qualifies as a "public record" under the content/context test of City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608, since it plainly relates to "the conduct of the public's business." Counsel would likely concede that the clerk's search was legally inadequate — San Jose requires an inquiry to individual officials about potentially responsive material on personal accounts/devices, not just a search of city systems — and would recommend promptly curing this by directing councilmembers to search personal devices, producing the 40 surviving screenshots (redacted as needed for privilege/deliberative process claims that counsel would likely assert are weak but worth raising), and issuing a supplemental CPRA response rather than defending the "no responsive records" answer through litigation. On the deleted messages, counsel would resist framing auto-deletion as a knowing/willful § 34090 violation (arguing the retention schedule's applicability to individual officials' personal-device ephemeral messaging is legally unsettled and the councilmembers' conduct was not city action), while still opening a litigation hold now, quietly documenting good faith, and treating any adverse-inference or spoliation argument in the franchise litigation as a settlement-pressure factor rather than something to fight to a published ruling. Counsel would likely recommend settling/mooting the CPRA suit (supplemental production, stipulated declaratory relief, negotiated fees under Gov. Code § 7923.115) rather than litigating San Jose's scope to judgment, refer the three-councilmember group chat for a quiet internal Brown Act compliance review (without volunteering a serial-meeting admission), and adopt going forward a personal-device/ephemeral-messaging and records-retention policy — all aimed at containing exposure and optics rather than vindicating the city's original "no responsive records" position.
